title: SEGMENT 7 — Thomas Hunt Morgan and the chromosomal theory of inheritance

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Thomas Hunt Morgan and the chromosomal theory of inheritance

If the worm was the genetic organism of the late twentieth century, the fly — Drosophila melanogaster, the common fruit fly — was the genetic organism of the entire twentieth century, and its dominance started in 1908 in a small, cluttered, banana-smelling room at Columbia University in New York, where a zoologist named Thomas Hunt Morgan and his students began the experiments that would establish, for the first time, the physical basis of heredity.
Let me set the scene.
In 1900, Gregor Mendel's laws of inheritance had just been rediscovered.
Three independent investigators had stumbled across Mendel's 1866 paper on pea genetics and recognized that he had worked out the basic mathematical rules of how traits are inherited.
So by 1908, geneticists knew that there were discrete units of heredity — what would be called genes — that segregated cleanly in crosses and recombined in predictable ratios.
But nobody knew where in the cell these units lived.
There were theories — some people thought they were in the cytoplasm, some in some general kind of "hereditary substance" — but no proof.
The physical basis of heredity was an open question.
Morgan set out to attack the problem with flies.
Why flies?
For the same reasons Brenner picked worms half a century later, scaled to the technology of 1908.
One: flies breed fast.
A fly goes from egg to adult in about ten days, and a single female lays hundreds of eggs in her lifetime.
Compared to mice, rabbits, or guinea pigs — the other "small lab animals" of the era — flies provided dozens of generations per year and thousands of offspring per cross.
Two: flies are cheap.
They live in milk bottles with a thin layer of food at the bottom — yeast paste, banana mash, eventually a defined cornmeal-molasses medium.
You can keep a thousand flies in a bottle on a shelf.
The Morgan lab famously stank of overripe bananas, and that smell was the smell of a serious genetics enterprise running on essentially no budget.
Three: flies have clear morphology.
Eye color, wing shape, body color, bristle pattern — flies have many easily visible traits that can serve as genetic markers.
You can sort them under a low-power microscope.
You can score a thousand flies in an afternoon.
Four: flies have a manageable chromosome number.
Four pairs.
That's it.
Compared to the dozens of chromosomes in many other animals, four pairs is tractable.
You can keep track of them.
Morgan started by looking for visible variants — mutant flies with abnormal traits.
For the first two years, he found very little.
Then, in 1910, one of his students spotted a male fly with white eyes, in a population where every other fly had the normal red eye color.
That single white-eyed fly turned out to be one of the most important specimens in the history of genetics.
Here's what Morgan did with it.
He bred the white-eyed male to a red-eyed female.
All the F1 offspring had red eyes — so red was dominant.
When he bred those F1 flies to each other, the F2 generation showed a 3:1 ratio of red to white — standard Mendelian segregation for a recessive trait.
So white-eye was acting like a normal recessive mutation.
So far, so Mendelian.
But here's the twist that broke the case open.
Of the white-eyed flies in the F2, all of them were male.
None were female.
White-eye was inherited in a way that linked it tightly to the sex of the fly.
This was something Mendel had not seen and could not have predicted.
Morgan and his students worked out the explanation.
Flies, like mammals, have sex chromosomes — X and Y.
Females are XX, males are XY.
And the white-eye gene, Morgan inferred, must live on the X chromosome.
So females have two copies of it (one on each X), and a single normal copy is enough to give red eyes.
Males have only one copy (on their single X), and if that copy is mutant, the male has white eyes — there's no second copy to mask it.
This was the first concrete demonstration that a specific gene was located on a specific chromosome.
The white-eye gene was on X.
Genes were physical things, sitting on physical chromosomes, segregating according to the rules of chromosome segregation in meiosis.

The chromosomal theory of inheritance — the idea that chromosomes are the physical carriers of genetic information — had been proposed before, but Morgan's white-eye work was the first ironclad experimental evidence.

Over the next two decades, Morgan and his students — Alfred Sturtevant, Calvin Bridges, Hermann Muller, and many others — used the same logic to map dozens, then hundreds of fly genes to specific chromosomes and to specific positions on those chromosomes.

Sturtevant, as a Columbia undergraduate in 1913, was the one who realized that the frequency of recombination between two linked genes — how often they get separated during meiosis — could be used as a measure of how far apart they are on the chromosome.
Genes close together on the same chromosome get separated rarely; genes far apart get separated often.
So you could turn recombination frequency into a genetic map — a physical map of where genes sit along the chromosome — using nothing but crosses and counting offspring.
The Morgan group's fly room produced, by 1925, the first detailed genetic map of any animal.
It showed dozens of fly genes laid out along the four chromosomes, at measured distances from each other, in defined orders.
It was the beginning of genetic mapping as a science.
Every subsequent map of every genome — including the human genome — uses the recombination-frequency logic that Sturtevant worked out at age 19 in a fly room in 1913.

The analogy: the fly room was the chromosome school of the early twentieth century — Thomas Hunt Morgan's flies established that genes sit on chromosomes; this is where the textbook genetics came from.
The whole structure of how we think about inheritance — linkage, recombination, sex linkage, genetic maps — was worked out in flies between 1908 and 1925.
Morgan won the Nobel Prize in 1933 for it.
It is the foundational period of classical genetics, and almost every concept you learned in introductory biology about how genes behave traces back to those banana-smelling milk bottles at Columbia.
The big idea to leave this segment with: before molecular biology, the fly was the organism in which genetics became a quantitative science.
The chromosomal theory of inheritance, the genetic map, the concept of linkage, the rules of sex linkage — all of it came from Drosophila.
The fly was, in 1925, what the worm became in 1985: the small animal in which the experimental apparatus of genetics was forged.
Now let's look more closely at the actual mutants Morgan and his students were collecting, because the stories of those mutants tell you how fly genetics actually got done.
